items.caption: ONCE, LONG AGO, IN A VERY WEAK MOMENT I TOOK A JOB! A JOB!
items.md: ![ONCE, LONG AGO, IN A VERY WEAK MOMENT I TOOK A JOB! A JOB!](CBatAotCB-056_page_1_image_2_v2.jpg)

items.caption: YES! I ACTUALLY WORKED — I EARNED THAT DIME!
items.md: ![YES! I ACTUALLY WORKED — I EARNED THAT DIME!](CBatAotCB-056_page_1_image_1_v2.jpg)

items.caption: AND I'VE BEEN SO ASHAMED OF IT EVER SINCE THAT I HID IT IN THE SAFE AND NEVER LOOKED AT IT AGAIN!
items.md: ![AND I'VE BEEN SO ASHAMED OF IT EVER SINCE THAT I HID IT IN THE SAFE AND NEVER LOOKED AT IT AGAIN!](CBatAotCB-056_page_1_image_3_v2.jpg)

items.caption: ANY COMMENTS? NOTHING THAT COULD BE PRINTED IN THE PAPER!
items.md: ![ANY COMMENTS? NOTHING THAT COULD BE PRINTED IN THE PAPER!](CBatAotCB-056_page_1_image_4_v2.jpg)
From *Walt Disney's Comics* No. 140, May 1952; © 1952 Walt Disney Productions.
items.value: From Walt Disney's Comics No. 140, May 1952; © 1952 Walt Disney Productions.

items.md: tors seizes him and demands five dollars that Gladstone owes him. Gladstone sticks out his hand and into it floats — not a five, but a ten-dollar bill. Gladstone buys an old trunk for fifty cents at an auction and, sure enough, it contains a hundred dollars in a secret money cache. And so it goes.
items.value: tors seizes him and demands five dollars that Gladstone owes him. Gladstone sticks out his hand and into it floats — not a five, but a ten-dollar bill. Gladstone buys an old trunk for fifty cents at an auction and, sure enough, it contains a hundred dollars in a secret money cache. And so it goes.

items.md: Gladstone retained a certain hauteur even when his luck went sour (as it did at times, always temporarily). In the April 1954 *Walt Disney's Comics*, Donald has beaten Gladstone in a raffle drawing in full view of a large audience, and as the ducks and the crowd chortle over his defeat, Gladstone thinks gloomily: "How awful to be a deposed monarch! How terrible to sit in sackcloth and ashes while ragpickers cheer at my downfall."

items.md: It was in *Walt Disney's Comics* that the best stories with Gladstone appeared, because it
items.value: It was in Walt Disney's Comics that the best stories with Gladstone appeared, because it

items.md: was in them that his victories over the ducks could be palatable. In a long story like "Luck of the North," the ducks could not be left choking in Gladstone's dust; they had suffered for too many pages for that to be acceptable to young readers, even when their sufferings were self-inflicted.
items.value: was in them that his victories over the ducks could be palatable. In a long story like "Luck of the North," the ducks could not be left choking in Gladstone's dust; they had suffered for too many pages for that to be acceptable to young readers, even when their sufferings were self-inflicted.

items.md: The unstated message in most of Barks's stories about luck — those with and without Gladstone — was that hard work and honest effort are ends in themselves, and that life's rewards are not reserved for the meritorious. That is a pretty bleak statement for a comic book to make, and a number of Barks's stories about luck were confused and contradictory to some degree, as if he were trying to soften the implications of what was happening to his characters.
